questions.text: I would work a job I dislike if it meant my family or community benefited greatly.
I hold the view that the person who prepares the most is the one who succeeds.
I feel empowered when I can make executive decisions.
I feel things at a pitch that sometimes scares me.
I have a strong desire to leave my mark on the things I work on.
I tend to seek out roles and projects that will look good on my own record.
I believe that inspiration and intuition are more reliable guides than any process.
When I encounter injustice, my entire body lights up with agitation.
I feel that a democratic team outperforms a top-down one.
I rarely notice when someone is posturing for an audience during a meeting.
My emotional responses are present but proportionate — they don't take over.
I find deep satisfaction in checking items off a well-organized to-do list.
I believe that processing human hardship as data — rather than as personal experience — leads to more effective interventions.
I hold the view that the moral weight of suffering should not be discounted because the person is distant, unfamiliar, or different from me.
I find that I naturally track the undercurrents of group dynamics.
I notice when someone carefully chooses words to avoid offending a specific person in the room.
I hold the view that the seriousness with which I engage with others' suffering is the most authentic measure of whether I am living according to my values.
I prefer to let others take credit even when I did most of the work.
